intro: A heavy month for model launches collides with a rough stretch for bitcoin: spot ETFs are bleeding while AI coding tools quietly rewire how they bill you.

items.headline: Microsoft unveils MAI-Code-1-Flash, its first in-house coding model
items.summary: At its Build conference Microsoft introduced MAI-Code-1-Flash, a model that turns written descriptions into source code for apps and websites — part of a push to lean less on OpenAI.
items.why: The big platforms are now shipping their own coding models, not just reselling OpenAI's.

items.headline: Google ships Antigravity 2.0 with a new CLI and Gemini 3.5 Flash as default
items.summary: Announced at I/O on May 19, Antigravity 2.0 adds a command-line interface and makes Gemini 3.5 Flash the default; quota counters were reset to zero in early June.
items.why: Antigravity is Google's bid to be the agentic dev environment, not just an autocomplete.

items.headline: The shift from completion to delegation goes mainstream
items.summary: AWS, Google Cloud, GitHub, Databricks and others are now framing agents as a new software layer, with developers delegating whole tasks — issues, tests, migrations, refactors — rather than finishing lines.

items.headline: Anthropic launches Claude Fable 5 and Claude Mythos 5
items.summary: Claude Fable 5 lands as a safer general-purpose model with stronger coding, vision, memory and long-context performance; Claude Mythos 5 arrives for trusted-access users with new safeguards.

items.headline: Microsoft debuts new in-house models to cut OpenAI reliance and dev costs
items.summary: Microsoft unveiled a family of new AI models aimed at lowering costs for developers and reducing its dependence on OpenAI.
items.why: A notable strategic crack in the Microsoft–OpenAI alliance.

items.headline: Gemini 3.5 Flash goes GA, becomes default in the Gemini app and Search
items.summary: Google's Gemini 3.5 Flash shipped at I/O and is now the default in the Gemini app and AI Mode in Search, with API access priced at $1.50 / $9.00 per million tokens.

items.headline: June's launch wave: Grok 5, Gemini 3.5 Pro and a rumored Sonnet 4.8
items.summary: Four model storylines landed in four weeks — Google's Gemini 3.5 Pro, Anthropic's Mythos line, the long-delayed Grok 5 from xAI, and a rumored Claude Sonnet 4.8.

items.headline: GitHub Copilot moves to usage-based billing
items.summary: As of June 1, Copilot replaced Premium Request Units with token-metered AI Credits (1 credit = $0.01). Base prices are unchanged and completions stay unlimited, but chat, agentic sessions and code review now draw down credits — which don't roll over.
items.why: Heavy agentic users may pay more for the same base subscription; track your token spend.

items.headline: JetBrains releases Koog 1.0, an open-source AI-agent framework for the JVM
items.summary: Announced at KotlinConf 2026, Koog 1.0 provides building blocks for agentic apps in Kotlin and Java — tools, workflows, persistence, memory and observability — with Kotlin Multiplatform integration.
items.why: A first-class agent framework for teams that live in Kotlin/Java rather than Python.

items.headline: Bitcoin slides toward $61K as ETF outflows and a Strategy sale hit sentiment
items.summary: BTC fell over 6% in 24 hours on June 3 to an intraday low near $65,710 and drifted to roughly $61,000 by mid-month, pressured by spot-ETF outflows and a notable sale by Strategy (MSTR).
items.why: The corporate-treasury bid that propped up prior rallies is now a source of supply.

items.headline: Spot bitcoin ETFs post their largest outflow streak since launch
items.summary: A 12-day outflow streak totaled $3.58 billion — the biggest weekly exodus since the products debuted in January 2024.

items.headline: SEC fast-tracks an actively managed bitcoin income ETF
items.summary: On June 3 the SEC granted accelerated approval for Nasdaq to list the iShares Bitcoin Premium Income ETF, which holds bitcoin, IBIT shares and cash while writing call options for income.
items.why: Product innovation continues even as flows turn negative — a sign of a maturing ETF market.

items.headline: Crypto regulation advances as banks push back
items.summary: The Clarity Act cleared the Senate Banking Committee and the CFTC moved to greenlight higher-leverage derivatives for U.S. institutions, but banking leaders including Jamie Dimon opposed letting crypto firms operate like banks without equivalent consumer protections.
